original_abstract:
Building upon the discussion of wisdom formation in Sir 6:18-22, the second part of the pericope (vv. 23-31) shifts its focus toward the total submission required to attain wisdom and the transformative effects of that surrender. This study demonstrates that Sir 6:23-31 is organized around a concentric structure centered on verses 26-28. 

The outer layers of this structure reveal a deliberate thematic progression. The first section (vv. 23-25) utilizes the harsh imagery of servitude—chains, collars, and yokes—to emphasize that the seeker must relinquish personal freedom and embrace the rigorous hardships inherent in the pursuit of wisdom. In contrast, the third section (vv. 29-31) alludes to these same images of enslavement but reinterprets them as symbols of a glorious future. What were once viewed as restrictive bonds are transformed into "robes of glory" and "crowns of joy," representing the highest social and spiritual distinctions available to the sage.

Between these sections, the central pivot (vv. 26-28) employs the imagery of hunting to underscore the necessity of a persistent, active search that engages the soul across every sphere of life. Ultimately, the sage argues that full psychological and spiritual yielding to wisdom—akin to the devotion of a slave to a master—is the only path to the "rest" and happiness wisdom provides. By moving from the "servitude" of the apprentice to the "royalty" of the accomplished sage, Sirach illustrates that the temporary loss of autonomy is the prerequisite for eternal joy and honor.
humanized_abstract:
The second part of the pericope continues the theme of wisdom formation which began in Sir 6:18-22. The present study argues that the second part of the pericope (Sir 6:23-31) revolves around the theme of the complete surrender required for the acquisition of wisdom and its consequences. It is organized in a concentric structure with the center consisting of verses 26-28.

Looking to the outer layer of this structure, we find a progression of thought. The first section in this scheme is vv. 23-25, and it employs imagery that is both extremely unpleasant and very directly associated with slavery (e.g., “links of iron,” “bands of affliction,” “yoke upon the neck”). The idea is that the seeker must sacrifice his or her freedom, accepting all the hardships that accompany the search for wisdom. The third section of the structure (vv. 29-31) takes up the same imagery, but reads it in a different sense. “Links of iron” and “bands of affliction” and “yoke upon the neck” appear, but this time as foreshadowing the glorious robes of honor and the precious crown of gladness to which the wise man will be advanced.

Between these two sections the central part (vv. 26-28) presents the image of the hunt, in order to stress the need for constant psychic vigilance in every respect. The sage concludes by affirming that only by renouncing the total psychic and spiritual surrender that slaves have for their masters is it possible to achieve the “rest” which wisdom promises. The progression from the “servitude” of the pupil to the “royalty” of the wise man makes clear that the temporary sacrifice of freedom is paid in exchange for eternal joy and honor.
